name: thiserror-expert
description: Provides guidance on creating custom error types with thiserror, including proper derive macros, error messages, and source error chaining. Activates when users define error enums or work with thiserror.
qualityReason: Highly specific and valuable Rust expertise
capabilities: Detects custom error enum definitions and suggests thiserror patterns
Recommends proper #[error("...")] attribute usage for clear error messages
Guides on using #[from] for automatic From trait implementations
Shows how to use #[source] for error chain preservation
Provides best practices for layered error architectures
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged pattern matches that are false positives. The 'backticks' detected are Rust attribute syntax (e.g., #[error("...")]) in documentation code examples, not shell commands. The skill contains only educational content and Rust code patterns for the thiserror crate.
valueStatement: Thiserror is a Rust crate that simplifies custom error type creation through derive macros. This skill provides expert guidance on defining error enums with clear messages, chaining source errors, and implementing proper From conversions for ergonomic error handling.
userTitle: Create Rust Error Types with Thiserror
limitations: Does not generate full error implementations outside thiserror patterns
Does not handle error logging or reporting libraries
Does not provide runtime error recovery strategies
Does not integrate with specific framework error types

useCases.targetUser: Rust developers refactoring error handling code
useCases.title: Migrate Manual Error Implementations
useCases.description: Convert verbose manual Display and Error trait implementations to concise thiserror derive macros with automatic trait implementations.

useCases.title: Design Application Error Types
useCases.description: Create well-structured error enums for applications with clear error variants, field-based messages, and proper source error chaining.

useCases.targetUser: Library and application developers integrating external crates
useCases.title: Integrate External Error Types
useCases.description: Wrap errors from libraries like std::io, sqlx, reqwest using #[from] to enable the ? operator for automatic conversion.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Error Conversion
promptTemplates.prompt: I have this error enum with manual Display implementation. Can you convert it to use thiserror?
promptTemplates.scenario: User has custom error code with manual Display trait

promptTemplates.title: Error with Source Chaining

promptTemplates.scenario: User needs to chain errors from standard library

promptTemplates.title: Error Conversion with Context
promptTemplates.scenario: User needs to add custom context while still using #[from] conversion
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to convert an io::Error to my error type but add custom message and file path context

promptTemplates.title: Layered Error Architecture
promptTemplates.prompt: What's the best way to structure errors across domain, infrastructure, and application layers with proper conversion between them?

bestPractices: Use descriptive field placeholders like {0}, {field_name} in error messages to include runtime values
Apply #[from] for errors that should support automatic ? operator conversion
Choose #[source] over #[from] when you need custom construction with added context
antiPatterns: Using vague error messages like 'Error occurred' without actionable information
Forgetting #[source] or #[from] when wrapping other error types
Using #[from] when custom error construction with additional fields is needed

faq.question: What is thiserror and why should I use it?
faq.answer: Thiserror is a Rust derive crate that automatically implements std::error::Error and Display traits for error enums. It reduces boilerplate code and ensures consistent error handling across your codebase.

faq.question: When should I use #[from] vs #[source]?
faq.answer: Use #[from] when you want automatic From trait implementation and the ? operator to work. Use #[source] when you need custom error construction with additional fields while still preserving the error chain.

faq.question: What is #[error(transparent)] used for?
faq.answer: #[error(transparent)] forwards all Display and source behavior to the inner error type. It is useful for wrapper errors that should not change how the inner error is displayed.

faq.question: How do I include field values in error messages?
faq.answer: Use {0}, {1} for positional fields in tuple variants. Use {field_name} for named fields in struct variants. The placeholders are replaced at runtime with actual values.

faq.question: Can thiserror work with generic error types?
faq.answer: Yes, thiserror supports generic error types. You can create error enums with type parameters as long as the type bounds include std::error::Error.

faq.question: Does thiserror support conditional compilation?
faq.answer: Yes, you can use #[cfg(feature = "...")] attributes on error variants to include or exclude them based on enabled features.
